preamble: The shift from an alveolar nasal [n] to a retroflex nasal [ɳ] (or indirectly to a retroflex stop [ʈ]) is known as retroflexion. It is a form of progressive assimilation where the tongue tip is pulled back, usually triggered by a preceding rhotic [r] or retroflex sibilant [ʂ].

examples.note: Dravidian languages contrast dental /n/ (ந) with retroflex /ṇ/ (ண) as phonemically distinct. Tamil 'eṇ' (eight) vs 'en' (my) — a lexical distinction absent in most non-Dravidian languages.
Nati: The dental nasal regularly retroflexes when preceded by r or ʂ (even at a distance).
In Central and Northern dialects, the /r/ + /n/ cluster merges into a single retroflex nasal.
